audioscript.text: Section 1, Gavin, a newly arrived student, wants to insure the contents of his apartment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: He calls an insurance agent to ask for information.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Diane Davies.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Yes, I'd like to get some insurance for the contents of my home.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Uh. When did you move into the house?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: A couple of weeks ago, and it's an apartment actually.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: I was told by the landlord that it would be a good idea to get some insurance for the furniture and other personal possessions.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Gavin moved into his apartment 2 weeks ago, so B has been circled.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the conversation and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Diane Davies.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Yes, I'd like to get some insurance for the contents of my home.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Uh. When did you move into the house?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: A couple of weeks ago, and it's an apartment actually.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: I was told by the landlord that it would be a good idea to get some insurance for the furniture and other personal possessions.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Well, uh, let's get some details.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: What kind of apartment is it?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: It's a 2 bedroom apartment.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Huh, uh, what floor is it on?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Um. Why do you need to know that?
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Because it affects the cost of the insurance.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: An apartment on the ground floor isn't as protected as others.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: And there's more chance of a break-in.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: I didn't know that.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Um. It's on the third, no, uh, 2nd floor.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Second...
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: and how much is the rent?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: It's $615 per month.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: And where is it located?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: In Biggins Street, South Hills.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: I see, and what things did you want to insure?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Well, what do you recommend?
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Well, uh, the most important things are those which you would normally find in a home.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Things like the television, fridge, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Well, I've got a fridge and a stereo system which I've just bought from a friend.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: And how much did you pay for the fridge?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Uh, so, 50 or 15?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: And the stereo system cost $1,150.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Uh. Have you got a television?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Yes, but it's very old, and not worth much.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: OK, uh, well, is there anything else you want to insure?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Yes, I've got a couple of watches and my CDs and books.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: How much do you think they're worth?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: The watches are worth $1,000.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: For both of them?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: No, each one, and all together, the CDs and books cost me about $400.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: OK, so the value of everything you want to insure is $4,000.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Hmm, how much will the insurance cost?
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Let me see $4,000 divided by plus 10% right, so, this kind of insurance, uh, that's private contents insurance.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Um. It comes to yeah, $184 for a 12 month period.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: $184 well, that sounds pretty good.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: OK, I'll take that policy.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Before the conversation continues, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Can I arrange the policy over the phone?
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Sure, just let me get the details down.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: So, that's Mr Gavin Murray.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: That's MURRAY.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: And the address is 16C.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Biggins Street, South Hills.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: OK, 16C, Biggins Street.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: South Hills.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: It's two words, 'South Hills'.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: And your date of birth is?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: 12 of November 1980.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: And your contact number?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Home phone number is 98724855.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Right, and you're Australian?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: No, I was born in London, although my mother is from Tasmania.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Oh, really?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Whereabouts?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Hobart.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Interesting place.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: Now, are you working at the moment?
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: I'm a full-time student at Sydney University.
audioscript.speaker: GAVIN

audioscript.text: Right, good.
audioscript.speaker: DIANE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

The monthly rent for Gavin's apartment is

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear an address by the college president, welcoming new students to a residential college.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first part of the talk, and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: It's good to see you all here.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Welcome to Smith House.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Smith House, as you may or may not know, is one of the oldest residential colleges of the university.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: As you can see, the building you're in now, which contains this main lounge, the dining room, the recreation room, the kitchen, and the offices, was part of the original old house built in the 1840s to be used by the family of George Smith.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: That's of course how the house and college got their names.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: The original house was converted into a residential college for the university in 1940 and since then has continued to be added on to and modernized.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: You'll notice when you receive your room allocation in a few minutes that your room number either begins with the letter N, S or W, like this one here.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: The first letter refers to the three wings of the college, which come away from this main building.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Of course the letters represent the three directions, in this case, north, south, and west.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Each wing has two floors, and so the next number you see is either 1 or in this case 2.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: And this indicates which floor your room is on.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: The number after that is your individual room number.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: So it's quite simple to find any room by going to the right wing.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Then floor and then room number.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll also notice when you receive your orientation pack shortly, that there are two keys.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: One is the key to your room, and only you have that key, and the other is a key to the front door, which you've just come through here from the street.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: This door is closed and locked at 8:00 pm every night, and opened again at 7:00 am.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: You'll need your key if you're coming back to the college between those times.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: We ask all students to always enter and leave the college through the front door.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: You'll notice at the end of each corridor that there is another door, but these are fire doors, and they're kept locked from the outside.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: They should only be opened from the inside in case of emergency.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: In your fees, you've paid a laundry fee which covers the cleaning of bedlinen and towels.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: All bedlinen and towels are clearly embossed with the name Smith House, so it's easily identifiable.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: If you want your other laundry to be done by the college, this can be arranged for a small extra fee.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: There are only a few rules here at Smith House, and we have these rules so that we can all live comfortably together.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: The most important rule is that there must be no noise after 9:00 pm.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: There is also no smoking in the rooms or anywhere inside the college, but smoking is permitted on the balconies.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: All meals are served in the dining room, meal times are listed in your orientation pack.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Please read these carefully, as meal times cannot be changed, and if you arrive late, I'm sorry to say you just go hungry.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: If you're unsure about things, each floor has an elected floor senior, who is usually a student in their 3rd or 4th year of study, who's been at Smith House for a while.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: The floor seniors will introduce themselves later today, and answer any questions you have.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: But for now, I'm going to hand you over to Marney, who is going to give you the orientation packs and keys.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: Thanks Marney.
audioscript.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: All bedlinen and towels are clearly embossed with the name Smith House, so it's easily identifiable.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: If you want your other laundry to be done by the college, this can be arranged for a small extra fee.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: There are only a few rules here at Smith House, and we have these rules so that we can all live comfortably together.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: The most important rule is that there must be no noise after 9:00 pm.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: There is also no smoking in the rooms or anywhere inside the college, but smoking is permitted on the balconies.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: All meals are served in the dining room, meal times are listed in your orientation pack.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: Please read these carefully, as meal times cannot be changed, and if you arrive late, I'm sorry to say you just go hungry.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: If you're unsure about things, each floor has an elected floor senior, who is usually a student in their 3rd or 4th year of study, who's been at Smith House for a while.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: The floor seniors will introduce themselves later today, and answer any questions you have.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: But for now, I'm going to hand you over to Marney, who is going to give you the orientation packs and keys.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: Thanks Marney.
utterances.speaker: COLLEGE PRESIDENT

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Smith House was originally built as

text: Complete the explanation of the room number.

items: Students need a front door key between <input[]> AND <input[]>.
In an emergency, students should use <input>.
No changes can be made to <input>
If you have any questions, ask the <input>
body.title: HOUSE RULES

answer_key.correct_answer: floor | floor number | 2nd floor | second floor | 2nd floor number | second floor number
floor senior | floor seniors | elected floor senior | elected floor seniors

test_text.title: Planning an Essay on Children Learning through Media

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a conversation between two students, Lynne and Robin, who are discussing an assignment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the conversation and answer questions 21 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That essay we have to write, the one on how children learn through the media.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: How are you planning to write it?
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Well, I've given it some thought, and I think that the best way to approach it is to divide the essay into two parts.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: First of all, we'd have to look at some examples of each type of media.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Yes, what they are.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Then we could describe how we can use each medium so that children can learn something from each one.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Maybe we could draw up a table and look at examples of each medium in turn.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Hmm, uh. Let's see, um, the different forms of media would be the print media.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Here you'd have things like books and newspapers that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Hmm. And included in these are the pictorial forms of print media, like maps.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Yes, maps are really just formal pictures, aren't they?
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Hmm. And then there are what we call the audio forms of media.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Where children can listen.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: CDs and radios are probably the best examples, because a lot of children have access to these, especially radios.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: And this would lead into the audio-visual media, which can be seen as well as heard.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Uh. Film, television, and we mustn't forget videos.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Yes, but there's a final category as well.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Computers, that make up the so called electronic media.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: In the United Kingdom and Australia, they say that one in three families has a computer now.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Yes, I believe it.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Well, uh, that's a good list to start with.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: We're really getting somewhere with this essay now.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: So, let's move on to when each type of medium could be used.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: I guess we could start by trying to identify the best situation for each type of media.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: What do you mean?
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Uh. I'm talking about whether each medium should be used with different sized groups.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: For example, we can look at pictures, and ask whether they're more useful for an individual child, a few children together or a full class.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: In this case, I'd say pictures are best with individual children, because they give them an opportunity to let their imaginations run wild.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Yes, I see.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Let's take tapes next.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Although tapes look ideal for individual children, I feel they're best suited to small group work.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Hmm. This way children don't feel isolated, cause they can get help from their friends.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Computers are the same.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: I think they're better with small numbers of children, and they're hardly ever useful with a whole class.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Videos, however, are ideal for use with everyone present in the class.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Especially when children have individual activity sheets to help them focus their minds on what's in the video.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: And what about books?
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: What would you recommend for them?
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Books are ideal for children to use by themselves.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: I know they're used with groups in schools, but I wouldn't recommend it.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Other pictorial media like maps though are different.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: I'd always plan group work around those.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Give the children a chance to interact and to share ideas.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Hmm, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Teachers often just leave maps on the wall for children to look at when they have some free time, but kids really enjoy using them for problem solving.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: Different people have different ideas, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: LYNNE

audioscript.text: Yeah, and different teachers recommend different tools for different age groups.
audioscript.speaker: ROBIN

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.listTitle: According to the speakers, in which situation are the following media most useful?

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecturer talking to a group of students at the beginning of a course in rural development.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the talk, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I hope that this first session which I've called an introduction to British Agriculture will provide a helpful background to the farm visits you'll be doing next week.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: I think I should start by emphasizing that agriculture still accounts for a very important part of this country's economy.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: We are used to hearing the UK's society and economy described as being industrial or even post industrial, but we mustn't let this blind us to the fact that agriculture and its supporting industries still account for around 20% of our gross national product.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: This figure is especially impressive, I think, when you bear in mind how very small a percentage of the UK workforce is employed in agriculture.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: This is not a recent development.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: You'd have to go back to 1750 or so to find a majority of the workforce in this country working in agriculture.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: By the middle of the next century, in 1850 that is.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: It had fallen sharply to 10 percent, and then to 3% by the middle of the 20th century.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And now just 2% of the workforce contribute 20% of gnp.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: How is this efficiency achieved?
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Well, my own view is that it owes a great deal to a history over the last 50 or 60 years of intelligent support by the state.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Mainly taking the form of helping farmers to plan ahead.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Then the two other factors I should mention, both very important, are the high level of training amongst the agricultural workforce.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And secondly, the recognition by farmers of the value of investing in technology.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Now, although the UK is a fairly small country, the geology and climate vary a good deal from region to region.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: For our purposes today, we can divide the country broadly into three.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: I've marked them on the map here.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The region you'll get to know best of course is the north, where we are at present.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The land here is generally hilly, and the soils thin.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The climate up here, and you've already had evidence of this, is generally cool and wet.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: As you'll see next week, the typical farm here in the north is a small family run concern, producing mainly wool and timber for the market.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: If we contrast that with the eastern region over here, the east is flatter and more low lying with fertile soils and a mixed climate.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Average farm size is much bigger in the east.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And farms are likely to be managed strictly on commercial lines.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: As for crops, well, the east is the UK's great cereal producing region.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: However, increasingly significant areas are now also given over to high quality vegetables for supply direct to the supermarkets.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The third broad region is the west, where it's a different story again.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The climate is warmer than in the north, and much wetter than in the east.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The resulting rich soils in the west provide excellent pasture.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And the farms there are quite large, typically around 800 hectares.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The main products are milk, cheese and meat.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: So clearly there are marked differences between regions.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: But this does not prevent quite a strong sense of solidarity amongst the farming community as a whole, right across the country.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: This solidarity comes in part from the need to present a united front in dealing with other powerful interest groups.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Such as government or the media, it also owes something to the close cooperation between all the agricultural training colleges, through which the great majority of farmers pass at the beginning of their careers.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And a third factor making for solidarity is the national structure of the Farmers' Union, of which virtually all farmers are members.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Finally, in this short talk, I'd like to say a little about the challenges facing farmers in the next...
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What percentage of the workforce were employed in agriculture in the mid 1900s?

items: the quality of <input> among those employed
items.title: Three factors contributing to the efficiency of the agricultural sector are:
Farmers have a strong sense of solidarity because
